title: An Elegant Hairdo, They Say; Fragrant Screw-Pine Flowers, They Say! (3)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, legal findings, financial figures, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Allegations and findings remain attributed to the judgment and witnesses described in the letter. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy. Page images 116-122 were used to restore the opening salutation and the substantial passage omitted after `900 ஏக்கர்`, including the printed Rs. 7 crore, Rs. 10,000-per-acre and 3,000-acre figures. Mechanical OCR joins and damaged proper names were also repaired. The frozen source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: At the end of yesterday's letter, I said that I would point out what the Special Judge of the Bengaluru court had written in his judgment about former Chief Minister Jayalalithaa. Judge Cunha wrote:

“Before becoming a public servant, that is, before becoming Chief Minister, Jayalalithaa declared an income of Rs. 2 crore. The income of J.-Sasi Enterprises and Jaya Publications was included within that Rs. 2 crore. But the speed at which their income rose during her five years as Chief Minister is beyond imagination.

“A farm belonging to them extended over 900 acres. They submitted accounts claiming that it had been purchased for a mere Rs. 7 crore. Land could not have been bought at that price even at the minimum value fixed by the Government. It meant that an entire acre was shown as acquired for just Rs. 10,000. On that basis, a whole village could have been bought for Rs. 7 crore. Their companies collectively held 3,000 acres. Yet they could not state the income from those 3,000 acres. All their valuations were calculated according to Government-fixed rates and included in this case. Had market values been used, the total valuation would have been far greater than what we can now calculate.

“Others helped Jayalalithaa acquire these properties. They established company after company and used the bank accounts of inactive companies to help her accumulate assets. No one invested in those companies. No one purchased their shares. They carried on no commercial activity. Yet the companies belonged to them. Their bank accounts were used only to receive assets acquired beyond their income and convert those assets into property belonging to themselves. The arguments and evidence have established all this beyond doubt. The only transactions in these accounts were transfers of money needed to acquire and accumulate assets.

“Jayalalithaa committed these offences while holding the highest responsibility in the Tamil Nadu Government, as Chief Minister. That aggravates their seriousness. We must remember the maxim, ‘As the ruler goes, so go the people.’ Jayalalithaa showed the entire society, including the government machinery and institutions beneath her and the public dependent upon them, a wrongful path that could only cause harm.

“While sentencing the accused, I wish to recall the Karnataka High Court's ruling in *P. Subbaiah v. State of Karnataka*. It observed that showing leniency when a person holding high office commits wrongdoing can bring harm upon society as a whole.

“None of the reasons advanced by the accused in seeking mercy is reasonable or acceptable. There is no doubt that the case lasted eighteen years. There is no need now to inquire into who caused that delay. But the case was transferred to Bengaluru because efforts had been made to bury justice. After the transfer, it was the accused who wasted a great deal of time. No one can deny it. They delayed the proceedings and wasted the Court's time by raising matters that contributed nothing to the case and invoking legal procedures wholly unrelated to it.

“The sentence can be determined only from the gravity of the offence, the extent of the assets and the methods the accused followed to acquire them. The law permits a maximum sentence of seven years' imprisonment in this case. Justice will be served only if the accused receive at least more than half the maximum sentence allowed by law. I therefore sentence each accused to four years' imprisonment.”
Judge Cunha wrote this firmly in his judgment.

That is not all. Cunha also explained that Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi acquired money and property through improper means for Jayalalithaa, under her supervision and according to her wishes. The relevant passage reads:

“At the end of 1996, the assets held by Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi were valued at Rs. 55,02,48,215. During that period, they spent Rs. 8,49,06,833, making a total of Rs. 63,51,55,048. The income they declared was only Rs. 9,91,05,094. This Court therefore concludes that the unexplained amount was Rs. 53,60,49,954.

“Jayalalithaa and the others cannot claim this unexplained amount as inherited property. Before 1991, Jayalalithaa officially declared assets of only Rs. 2 crore. The prosecution has proved this clearly, and the documents filed by Sasikala and Ilavarasi themselves are sufficient to establish it.

“According to the testimony and documents of prosecution witness R. Krishnamurthy, Sasikala's sole source of income was her husband, Natarajan, a government employee. While serving as a Public Relations Officer, Natarajan obtained a Rs. 3,000 advance loan from the Tamil Nadu Government to buy a motorcycle for his personal use. In 1987, he bought a house with a government loan, for which clear records exist. That was the extent of the property owned by Natarajan and Sasikala. Natarajan resigned from government service in 1988. It is therefore established that Sasikala did not receive any substantial property through him. Nor did she receive substantial ancestral property from her father.

“A crucial fact is that Sasikala began filing income-tax returns only after she started living permanently with Jayalalithaa at Poes Garden. According to the testimony and documents of District Registrar Thangavelu, companies including J.S. Housing Development, J.J. Leasing, Green Farm House, J. Farm House, J. Real Estate, Jaya Contractors and Builders, Metal King and Marble Marvels were established by Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi while Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister. Two, three or all four of them were partners in each company.

“Thangavelu further testified that his superior, S. Iyer, now appointed by the AIADMK Government as State Election Commissioner, instructed him to register these companies, and that the Chief Minister had issued the instruction to Iyer by telephone.

“Ilavarasi and Sudhakaran signed and submitted the application to establish Sudhakaran's Super Duper TV company. The testimony of Commercial Taxes Officer Kannan confirms this. Similarly, Commercial Taxes Officer Sundararaj testified that Sasikala and Sudhakaran signed the application for Anjaneya Printers. They also signed the application to register Marble Marvels. It is thus established beyond doubt that they were the people who started and operated these companies.

“None of the four invested any amount in any of the companies they controlled. They did not raise capital by selling shares. No profit from the companies went to any account other than those of the four accused. The statement of Balaji, auditor to Sasikala and Ilavarasi, is particularly important. According to him, these companies never manufactured anything and never bought or sold goods. Yet money continued to move through their accounts.

“The prosecution submitted evidence concerning the companies' bank accounts and transactions. The records are astonishing. Shell companies with no capital and companies engaged in no business repeatedly conducted transactions involving thousands and lakhs of rupees. To circulate the money in this manner, Jayalalithaa and Sasikala, who had maintained only twelve bank accounts before 1991, opened fifty-two accounts afterward.

“The testimony of the North Beach Sub-Registrar and Horticulture Department Officer Radhakrishnan is highly significant. Acting on their superiors' orders, they were summoned to Jayalalithaa's Poes Garden residence and made to register documents without following the rules. All the lands covered by those deeds were registered at throwaway prices. Most shockingly, six deeds were registered without even naming or identifying the purchaser. When present possession of each property was examined, all were found to have been purchased for Jayalalithaa.

“There is no accounting for money deposited into the *Namadhu MGR* bank account on 423 separate occasions, each involving many thousands or lakhs of rupees. Jayalalithaa's side could offer no explanation for the crores deposited into Jaya Publications, Vinod Video Vision, Maha Subbulakshmi Kalyana Mandapam, Anjaneya Printers and other entities. Auditors and documentary evidence established that the balance sheets and profit-and-loss accounts produced by Jayalalithaa's side were fabricated.

“Prosecution witness Jayaraman confirmed that Sasikala directed the depositing of money into banks and its transfer to other accounts. Jayaraman was a car driver employed at Poes Garden. Sasikala repeatedly gave him bags and suitcases filled with lakhs of rupees together with completed bank challans and instructed him to deposit the money. He did so.

“In other words, they deposited the cash into accounts of companies they controlled, converted it into company funds, and then transferred it from the companies to bank accounts in their own names. Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi undertook these activities for Jayalalithaa.”
Udanpirappē, every one of these details comes from the judgment written by Judge Cunha. Though his judgment runs to 1,136 pages, I have selected and presented to you only its most important portions.
With affection,
M.K.
22 October 2014
